synopsis: A philosophical application of utilitarian ethics to animal welfare, global poverty, abortion, euthanasia, and obligations to future generations.
review: A philosophical primer on applied ethics. Singer's arguments are often uncomfortable and that discomfort is part of the point.

title: The Problems of Philosophy
author: Bertrand Russell
synopsis: A short philosophical introduction to epistemology and metaphysics, covering appearance and reality, induction, knowledge by acquaintance, and the value of philosophical enquiry.
review: A clear, compact philosophical starting point. Russell's writing is a useful reminder that philosophy can be rigorous without being impenetrable.

title: Language Truth and Logic
author: A. J. Ayer
synopsis: A philosophical manifesto for logical positivism, arguing that statements are meaningful only if analytic or empirically verifiable.
review: A philosophical period piece with lasting influence. Ayer's confidence has not aged well but the clarity of the verificationist programme is still instructive.

title: On Bullshit
author: Harry Frankfurt
synopsis: A short philosophical essay distinguishing bullshit, as indifference to truth, from lying, as deliberate deception, and analysing its role in modern public life.
review: A short and sharp philosophical essay. Frankfurt's distinction between lying and bullshit has entered common vocabulary for good reasons.

title: Software Engineering at Google
author: Titus Winters, Tom Manshreck, and Hyrum Wright
synopsis: A technical and engineering-focused account of software practice at Google, covering code review, testing, build systems, dependencies, and large-scale engineering culture.
review: A technical reference for engineers and tech leads. Less theoretical than most software books and much more grounded in real engineering organisation.

title: The Software Engineer's Guidebook
author: Gergely Orosz
synopsis: A practical technical guide for software engineers navigating career growth, from junior engineer to staff, with concrete advice on engineering skills and developer practice.
review: A technical and career-focused software engineering book. Orosz is direct about tech industry norms and the developer career ladders at big companies.

title: Kubernetes in Action
author: Marko Luksa
synopsis: A technical guide for developers and engineers to Kubernetes concepts, covering pods, services, deployments, stateful sets, and cluster operations.
review: A solid technical reference for software engineers deploying containerised applications. The worked examples are more useful than most Kubernetes documentation.

title: Terraform: Up and Running
author: Yevgeniy Brikman
synopsis: A technical and practical guide to infrastructure as code using Terraform, covering modules, state management, and deployment pipelines for software engineers.
review: A technical book aimed squarely at working software engineers. Brikman's examples reflect real tech-company infrastructure patterns.

title: Building Microservices
author: Sam Newman
synopsis: A technical book on software architecture for microservices, covering service decomposition, deployment, testing, security, and organisational aspects of microservice systems.
review: A careful technical treatment of microservice software architecture. Newman is realistic about the costs and not just the benefits, which many developer books are not.

title: Site Reliability Engineering
author: Google
synopsis: A technical collection of essays on Google's approach to software reliability, covering service-level objectives, error budgets, incident response, and engineering culture.
review: A technical and organisational reference for software reliability engineers. The chapters on error budgets and on-call are the most widely cited for good reason.

title: The DevOps Handbook
author: Gene Kim, Jez Humble, Patrick Debois, John Willis
synopsis: A technical and managerial guide to applying DevOps principles in software engineering organisations, covering deployment, testing, monitoring, and team practice.
review: A technical practitioner guide for software engineers and engineering managers. The case studies across several tech companies make it concrete.

title: Grokking Algorithms
author: Aditya Bhargava
synopsis: A technical and illustrated introduction to algorithms for software developers, covering search, sorting, graphs, dynamic programming, and hashing with Python examples.
review: A gentle technical introduction aimed at working software developers rather than computer science students. The illustrations do a lot of the teaching.

title: Database Internals
author: Alex Petrov
synopsis: A technical deep dive into database storage engines and distributed systems for software engineers, covering B-trees, LSM trees, consensus, and replication.
review: A strong technical reference for backend software engineers. Petrov treats storage engines and distributed systems as two halves of the same subject.

title: SQL Performance Explained
author: Markus Winand
synopsis: A technical guide for developers on SQL performance, focused on indexes, execution plans, and query tuning across major relational databases.
review: A compact technical book every working software developer who touches a database should read. Winand's treatment of indexes alone is worth the cover price.

title: High Performance Browser Networking
author: Ilya Grigorik
synopsis: A technical reference on web performance covering TCP, TLS, HTTP, WebSockets, and mobile networks, aimed at front-end and full-stack developers.
review: A technical reference for any software engineer serious about web performance. The TCP and TLS chapters in particular hold up better than most web-performance books.

title: System Design Interview
author: Alex Xu
synopsis: A technical interview preparation book for software engineers covering large-scale system design problems, including URL shorteners, news feeds, rate limiters, and chat systems.
review: A technical interview prep book for working software developers and engineers. The worked designs are more useful as learning tools than they are as interview answers.

title: The Player of Games
author: Iain M. Banks
synopsis: A Culture game-player is sent to infiltrate a brutal empire whose entire social hierarchy is determined by a single extraordinarily complex board game.
review: My favourite entry point into the Culture. Iain M. Banks builds a whole civilisation around one game and the political satire cuts deep.

title: Use of Weapons
author: Iain M. Banks
synopsis: A Culture special-circumstances agent with a traumatic past is pulled out of retirement for one more mission, told in two alternating timelines that converge at a horrifying revelation.
review: Structurally audacious and emotionally brutal. Iain M. Banks's best Culture novel and one of the finest science fiction books ever written.

title: Ubik
author: Philip K. Dick
synopsis: A team of psychic-countering operatives is caught in a reality that seems to be decaying backwards through time, and only a mysterious spray-can product may hold the answer.
review: Philip K. Dick at his strangest. Reality comes apart in the usual Dickian way and the novel leaves you unsure what was real at any level.

title: A Scanner Darkly
author: Philip K. Dick
synopsis: An undercover narcotics agent in a near-future California is assigned to investigate himself, his personality slowly fractured by the brain-splitting drug at the centre of his case.
review: Philip K. Dick drawing on his own experiences with the drug culture. Paranoid, funny, and heartbreaking by the end.

title: Rendezvous with Rama
author: Arthur C. Clarke
synopsis: A vast alien cylinder enters the solar system and a human expedition has a few weeks to explore its interior before it swings around the sun and leaves forever.
review: Arthur C. Clarke at his most patient and descriptive. The novel is really an exploration narrative and the mystery is the point.

title: The Caves of Steel
author: Isaac Asimov
synopsis: A human detective in an overcrowded future New York teams up with a humanoid robot partner to investigate the murder of a prominent spacer ambassador.
review: Isaac Asimov fusing the robot stories with a classic detective puzzle. The world-building of the covered cities is as interesting as the mystery.

title: Foundation and Empire
author: Isaac Asimov
synopsis: The second Foundation novel pits the precarious colony against the Mule, a mutant whose existence was never accounted for in the original psychohistorical plan.
review: Isaac Asimov at his grand-strategy best. The Mule is one of science fiction's great disruptions to a confident plan.

title: Dune Messiah
author: Frank Herbert
synopsis: Frank Herbert's sequel to Dune follows the emperor Paul Muad'Dib dealing with the catastrophic consequences of the jihad he unleashed across the galaxy.
review: A deliberate anti-sequel to Dune. Frank Herbert pulls the rug out from the hero's journey and the book is more interesting for it.

title: Emma
author: Jane Austen
synopsis: Jane Austen's novel of a well-off young Englishwoman convinced of her matchmaking genius who bungles the romantic lives of everyone in her small village.
review: Jane Austen at her most socially observant. Emma is a harder heroine to like than Elizabeth Bennet and the novel is sharper for it.

title: Legion of the Damned
author: Rob Sanders
synopsis: A Warhammer 40k novel following the legendary Legion of the Damned and the planetary defenders they reinforce against a chaos cult and necron incursion.
review: Grim 40k fiction with the usual chaos cultists, space marines, and a kill team struggling against the void. Entertaining if you know the setting.

title: The Climbing Mount Improbable
author: Richard Dawkins
synopsis: Richard Dawkins's scientific explanation of how complex biological adaptations such as the eye and the wing can evolve by small selective steps up the slope of fitness.
review: Richard Dawkins as biology teacher. The central image of climbing the easy side of a mountain is a useful intuition pump for natural selection.

title: River Out of Eden
author: Richard Dawkins
synopsis: A short scientific book by Richard Dawkins on the gene's-eye view of evolution, genetic information, and the non-random survival of replicators.
review: Short and accessible Dawkins. The DNA-as-digital-river metaphor drives the scientific exposition.

title: Unweaving the Rainbow
author: Richard Dawkins
synopsis: Richard Dawkins defends the scientific worldview against charges that it diminishes wonder, arguing that understanding nature deepens rather than destroys its beauty.
review: Richard Dawkins at his least polemical. A scientific essay on awe that works better than you might expect from the author of The God Delusion.

title: Helgoland
author: Carlo Rovelli
synopsis: Carlo Rovelli's relational interpretation of quantum mechanics, told through the history of Heisenberg's summer on a remote North Sea island and the physics that followed.
review: Carlo Rovelli's characteristic blend of scientific exposition and lyrical prose. His relational view of quantum mechanics is idiosyncratic but well argued.

title: In Search of Schrodinger's Cat
author: John Gribbin
synopsis: John Gribbin's popular scientific history of quantum mechanics, covering the founders, the paradoxes, and the interpretations from Copenhagen to many-worlds.
review: John Gribbin makes quantum mechanics feel historically situated. A scientific classic of popular physics.

title: The Scientists
author: John Gribbin
synopsis: John Gribbin's scientific history of the last 500 years of scientific thought organised around the biographies of the scientists who drove it.
review: A scientific doorstop that doubles as a biographical history. John Gribbin is more sympathetic to some figures than others and the book is the better for it.

title: How To
author: Randall Munroe
synopsis: Randall Munroe applies overcomplicated scientific reasoning to everyday problems such as digging a hole, moving house, or catching a drone, in the style of xkcd's What If.
review: Exactly the scientific silliness you would expect from Randall Munroe. Curiosity-inspiring nonsense with real physics underneath.

title: Thing Explainer
author: Randall Munroe
synopsis: Randall Munroe explains complicated scientific and engineering objects, from the Saturn V to a dishwasher, using only the thousand most common English words.
review: A scientific illustration project as much as a book. Randall Munroe's constrained vocabulary produces oddly clarifying results.

title: The Better Angels of Our Nature
author: Steven Pinker
synopsis: Steven Pinker's scientific history of declining violence across human history, drawing on psychology, statistics, and historical sociology.
review: Steven Pinker at his most data-heavy. The scientific case for long-term decline in violence is contested on detail but the overall trend is real.

title: Rationality
author: Steven Pinker
synopsis: Steven Pinker on the scientific study of reason, covering logic, probability, game theory, and the cognitive biases that interfere with clear thinking.
review: A scientific survey aimed at a broad audience. Steven Pinker is a good explainer of formal rationality tools and the chapters on Bayesian reasoning are solid.

title: Mother Tongue
author: Bill Bryson
synopsis: Bill Bryson's popular scientific tour of the English language, covering its history, vocabulary, spelling, dialects, and peculiarities.
review: Bill Bryson on linguistics. Scientifically not always watertight but relentlessly curious and entertaining.

title: At Home
author: Bill Bryson
synopsis: Bill Bryson walks around his own Norfolk rectory and uses each room as a springboard for the scientific and social history of domestic life.
review: Bill Bryson's scientific and historical curiosity applied to the kitchen, the cellar, the bedroom. A tour that could have been gimmicky and is not.

title: The Grand Design of Nature
author: Joe Schwarcz
synopsis: A popular scientific tour of chemistry in everyday life, covering food science, cleaning, medicine, and the chemistry of materials we take for granted.
review: A scientific popular tour by a working chemist. The chapters on food chemistry and pharmacology are the strongest.

title: The Places in Between
author: Rory Stewart
synopsis: Rory Stewart's account of walking across Afghanistan from Herat to Kabul in early 2002, shortly after the fall of the Taliban, encountering villagers and history along the way.
review: Rory Stewart is a stronger travel writer than almost all of his contemporaries. The book is political history told on foot.

title: The Marches
author: Rory Stewart
synopsis: Rory Stewart walks the border between England and Scotland with his ageing father, folding personal memoir into a political and historical account of the borderlands.
review: Rory Stewart mixing memoir and political history. The relationship with his father is the emotional engine and the politics follows.

title: How to be a Liberal
author: Ian Dunt
synopsis: Ian Dunt's political and historical defence of liberalism as a political tradition, tracing its philosophical roots and applying them to contemporary British politics.
review: A political polemic with a historical spine. Dunt is a journalist first and the book is stronger for being readable than most political theory.

title: Fake Law
author: The Secret Barrister
synopsis: The Secret Barrister follows up their previous political expose of the British legal system with a book attacking the misrepresentations of law in the press and by politicians.
review: An expose of political distortions of the legal system in the UK. The Secret Barrister writes with real anger and concrete evidence.

title: Nothing Like It in the World
author: Stephen E. Ambrose
synopsis: Stephen E. Ambrose's political and industrial history of the building of the American transcontinental railroad in the nineteenth century.
review: A popular political and business history. Ambrose is strong on logistics and corruption and the scale of the engineering effort.

title: The Great Degeneration
author: Niall Ferguson
synopsis: Niall Ferguson's political and institutional argument that western economic and political institutions are in decline relative to their earlier promise.
review: Niall Ferguson writing political economy. His conservative political framing is explicit and the historical sweep is broader than the argument requires.

title: Talking Politics
author: David Runciman
synopsis: David Runciman's political essays and commentary on modern democracy, drawn from his podcast, covering populism, technology, and the fragility of democratic institutions.
review: Political and historical commentary from a political scientist. Runciman is calmer than most contemporary political writers and the book is stronger for it.

title: This Is Not Normal
author: William Davies
synopsis: William Davies's political and sociological essays on British and American political turbulence since 2016, covering Brexit, Trump, and the economic background to both.
review: Political sociology about contemporary political turmoil. Davies is a sharp political writer and the essays hold up better than most in the genre.

title: Rebel Ideas
author: Matthew Syed
synopsis: Matthew Syed's political and organisational argument for cognitive diversity, drawing on case studies from intelligence agencies, sports teams, and corporate boards.
review: A political and organisational pop-non-fiction book. Some of the case studies are over-simplified but the core political argument about groupthink is sound.

title: Back Story
author: David Mitchell
synopsis: David Mitchell's comic memoir of his life and political opinions, framed as a walk across London, covering his childhood, Cambridge, and his career in British comedy.
review: David Mitchell being David Mitchell. Witty and angry about British politics in equal measure.

title: Thinking About Thinking About It
author: David Mitchell
synopsis: A collection of David Mitchell's political and cultural newspaper columns, following up on his earlier collection, ranging across British public life.
review: David Mitchell at column length. Political and sarcastic and more thoughtful than the format usually allows.

title: How They Broke Britain
author: James O'Brien
synopsis: James O'Brien's political expose of the think tanks, press barons, and political actors who he argues have damaged British political and social life.
review: James O'Brien doing political journalism at book length. An expose with plenty of targets and plenty of sources.

title: How Not To Be Wrong
author: James O'Brien
synopsis: James O'Brien's political memoir of changing his own mind on several prominent positions over years of radio interviews and political argument.
review: James O'Brien more reflective than his usual polemical mode. A political book about the discipline of being wrong well.

title: Lying
author: Sam Harris
synopsis: Sam Harris's philosophical essay arguing that adults should almost never lie, even by polite omission, with worked examples from everyday life.
review: Sam Harris in short-form philosophical mode. The argument is pared down and the applications to ordinary life are where the philosophical work happens.

title: Hitch-22
author: Christopher Hitchens
synopsis: Christopher Hitchens's philosophical memoir of his political and intellectual life, from Oxford socialism through neoconservative breaks over Iraq, and the wit in between.
review: Christopher Hitchens's philosophical and political memoir. The sections on his mother and on leaving the left are the most personal.

title: Letters to a Young Contrarian
author: Christopher Hitchens
synopsis: Christopher Hitchens's philosophical letters to an imagined younger contrarian on the ethics and craft of dissent, argument, and intellectual independence.
review: Christopher Hitchens in epistolary philosophical mode. A short book of advice that doubles as a mini-autobiography of his intellectual positions.

title: The Book of Joy
author: The Dalai Lama and Desmond Tutu
synopsis: The Dalai Lama and Desmond Tutu spend a week in philosophical conversation on the nature of joy, suffering, and compassion across their Buddhist and Christian traditions.
review: Philosophical dialogue between two spiritual leaders. The Dalai Lama and Tutu genuinely disagree in interesting ways and the book is the better for it.

title: How to Be Compassionate
author: The Dalai Lama
synopsis: The Dalai Lama's philosophical guide to compassion as a Buddhist practice and a political and social virtue, with exercises for cultivating it.
review: Short philosophical Dalai Lama. The Buddhist framing is explicit and the exercises are practical rather than mystical.

title: The Daily Stoic
author: Ryan Holiday and Stephen Hanselman
synopsis: A philosophical daily reader of Stoic philosophy, pairing quotations from Epictetus, Seneca, and Marcus Aurelius with short contemporary reflections for each day of the year.
review: Stoic philosophy in daily doses. Holiday popularises more than he innovates, but the format works for building a Stoic philosophical habit.

title: Ego is the Enemy
author: Ryan Holiday
synopsis: A philosophical self-help book drawing on Stoic philosophy and historical biography to argue against ego as an obstacle to aspiration, success, and failure.
review: Ryan Holiday mining Stoic philosophy for self-help. The historical case studies are the strongest parts and the philosophical claims are modest.

title: The Good Life
author: Robert Waldinger and Marc Schulz
synopsis: A philosophical and scientific reflection on happiness drawing on the long-running Harvard Study of Adult Development, arguing that relationships are the strongest predictor of flourishing.
review: Philosophical and social-scientific at once. The empirical spine makes the philosophical arguments about flourishing more concrete than most.

title: Four Thousand Weeks
author: Oliver Burkeman
synopsis: Oliver Burkeman's philosophical meditation on time management built around the observation that a human life lasts about four thousand weeks and cannot be optimised away.
review: Philosophical anti-productivity writing. Burkeman is funny about the absurdities of productivity philosophy while still taking the underlying questions seriously.

title: Clean Coder
author: Robert C. Martin
synopsis: Robert C. Martin's technical and professional guide to software developer conduct, covering estimation, saying no, practice, and ethics for working programmers.
review: Robert C. Martin in professional-conduct mode. More opinionated than Clean Code and more argumentative, but with real technical craft underneath.

title: Agile Software Development
author: Robert C. Martin
synopsis: Robert C. Martin's technical book on agile principles, design patterns, and practices for writing maintainable object-oriented software.
review: Robert C. Martin on agile engineering. The technical chapters on SOLID principles and design patterns have aged better than the agile methodology chapters.

title: Emotional Design
author: Donald A. Norman
synopsis: Donald A. Norman's design and engineering book on why we form emotional attachments to things, covering visceral, behavioural, and reflective levels of design.
review: Donald A. Norman extending his usability framework into emotion. A good follow-up to The Design of Everyday Things for designers and engineers.

title: Living with Complexity
author: Donald A. Norman
synopsis: Donald A. Norman's design and engineering book arguing that complexity is inevitable in modern systems and that good design tames rather than removes it.
review: Donald A. Norman making peace with complexity in engineered systems. A quieter book than his earlier design classics but still full of concrete examples.

title: Thinking in Systems
author: Donella H. Meadows
synopsis: A technical and engineering introduction to systems thinking, covering feedback loops, stocks and flows, delays, and leverage points for intervention in complex systems.
review: A compact technical introduction to systems engineering thinking. Meadows is unusually clear for a systems theorist and the leverage points essay alone is worth it.

title: Code
author: Charles Petzold
synopsis: Charles Petzold's technical and engineering exposition of how computers work, building up from flashlights and telegraphs to CPUs and operating systems.
review: A technical classic of computer engineering explanation. Petzold builds understanding from nothing and the later chapters genuinely teach you how a CPU works.

title: Computer Architecture
author: John L. Hennessy and David A. Patterson
synopsis: A technical engineering textbook on computer architecture, covering instruction sets, pipelining, memory hierarchies, multiprocessors, and warehouse-scale computing.
review: The standard technical textbook for computer architecture courses. Hennessy and Patterson remain the default reference for processor engineering.

title: The Algorithm Design Manual
author: Steven S. Skiena
synopsis: A technical engineering reference on algorithm design for working programmers, covering data structures, graph algorithms, and a catalogue of algorithmic problems.
review: A practical technical reference aimed at software engineers rather than computer science undergraduates. The war stories make the engineering context concrete.

title: Version Control with Git
author: Jon Loeliger and Matthew McCullough
synopsis: A technical engineering reference on git, covering object model, branching, merging, rebasing, submodules, and administration for software developers.
review: A technical git reference for working software developers. Slightly dated on the hosted-service side but solid on the underlying object model and engineering.

title: Kanban
author: David J. Anderson
synopsis: David J. Anderson's technical and engineering guide to Kanban as an evolutionary approach to software engineering process change, with real case studies.
review: A technical and managerial engineering guide to Kanban. More rigorous than Scrum's foundational texts and focused on software engineering realities.

title: Architecting Modern Java EE Applications
author: Sebastian Daschner
synopsis: A technical engineering book on building modern Java EE applications, covering lightweight architectures, container deployment, testing, CDI, JAX-RS, and microservices patterns for Java developers.
review: A technical Java EE reference for working software engineers. Daschner focuses on pragmatic modern Java engineering rather than the heavyweight patterns older Java EE books are known for.

title: Moral Tribes
author: Joshua Greene
synopsis: A popular history and psychology of human cooperation, drawing on archaeology, evolutionary anthropology, and twentieth-century case studies of inter-group conflict to ask when morality works and when it fails.
review: Revisionist popular history of human nature in the Bregman register. The historical chapters on the tragedy of the commons and the twentieth-century case studies of cooperation across national lines are the strongest material.

title: The Undercover Economist
author: Tim Harford
synopsis: A popular economics book using everyday cases, from coffee shops to health insurance, to teach readers how markets, prices, and policy interact, with historical case studies throughout.
review: Harford's economic history of ordinary life. The chapters tracing the Industrial Revolution's pricing of labour and the twentieth-century history of rent control do real historical work alongside the economics.

title: How Not To Be Wrong: Making Sense of the Senseless
author: James O'Brien
synopsis: A radio presenter's memoir of changing his own mind on long-held political convictions, drawn from two decades of broadcasting and the history of late New Labour and early Tory Britain.
review: Journalistic memoir as recent political history. The reconstructed arguments with callers on Iraq, immigration, and the Blair years work as an oral history of British public opinion.

title: Dishonesty is the Second-Best Policy
author: David Mitchell
synopsis: A collected volume of a comedian's newspaper columns on British politics, manners, and the everyday absurdities of life under the Brexit-era Conservative government.
review: Column journalism as minor social history. The pieces on Theresa May's government and the early pandemic response have aged into period documents of recent British political history.

title: The Return of Depression Economics
author: Paul Krugman
synopsis: An economist's historical survey of financial crises from the Asian flu of 1997 through the Latin American debt episodes and the 2008 collapse, arguing that depression-era policy questions never went away.
review: Macroeconomic history in polemic form. Krugman's archival discussion of the Japanese lost decade and the Asian currency crisis is the strongest historical material.

title: Intellectuals and Society
author: Thomas Sowell
synopsis: A historical and economic critique of the influence of public intellectuals across the twentieth century, drawing on case studies of policy debates, court decisions, and social movements.
review: Sowell's historical case studies of twentieth-century intellectuals are the core of the book. The archival chapters on New Deal economists and Cold War foreign policy do the heaviest historical lifting.

title: Energy and Civilization: A History
author: Vaclav Smil
synopsis: A sweeping historical survey of human energy use from foraging societies through agricultural empires to the fossil-fuel revolution and the industrial century.
review: Popular history at its most quantitative. Smil's archival work on pre-industrial muscle power and the historical chapters on the steam engine and the electrification of cities are the spine of the book.

title: The Dawn of Everything
author: David Graeber and David Wengrow
synopsis: A revisionist history of humankind challenging the standard account of the agricultural revolution and the rise of the state, drawing on archaeology of the Americas, the Near East, and ancient Europe.
review: Sweeping popular history against the Harari grain. Graeber and Wengrow's archival use of Cahokia, Teotihuacan, and the indigenous critique of European civilisation makes this one of the more argumentative histories of recent years.

title: The Idea Factory: Bell Labs and the Great Age of American Innovation
author: Jon Gertner
synopsis: A reported history of Bell Labs across the twentieth century, tracing the scientists and engineers behind the transistor, the laser, information theory, and the corporate research culture that produced them.
review: A reported biography of an institution. Gertner's archival work on Shockley, Shannon, and the postwar research culture is first-rate corporate and scientific history.

title: The Voyage of the Beagle
author: Charles Darwin
synopsis: A young naturalist's journal of a multi-year Royal Navy voyage around South America and the Pacific, recording geology, flora, fauna, and encounters with indigenous peoples.
review: Primary historical source rather than theory. The chapters on Tierra del Fuego and the Galapagos are the ones that matter for the later biography of the theory of evolution.

title: The Righteous Mind
author: Jonathan Haidt
synopsis: A moral philosophy and psychology book arguing that intuition drives moral judgement and reason rationalises it, drawing on cross-cultural ethics, evolutionary psychology, and political philosophy.
review: A rigorous work of moral philosophy for a general audience. Haidt's treatment of the is-ought problem and the phenomenology of moral intuition sits alongside a genuine argument in ethics.

title: Consciousness: An Introduction
author: Susan Blackmore
synopsis: A philosophy of mind textbook surveying the hard problem of consciousness, the metaphysics of the self, and the contemplative traditions that have engaged with subjective experience.
review: A reliable first philosophy of mind book. Blackmore walks through the classical arguments on qualia, the split-brain philosophical literature, and the phenomenology of meditation with care.

title: Elbow Room: The Varieties of Free Will Worth Wanting
author: Daniel Dennett
synopsis: A philosophical defence of compatibilism about free will, arguing that a naturalistic account of ethics and moral responsibility does not require libertarian metaphysics.
review: Analytic philosophy of mind at its clearest. Dennett's argument against the Harris line on free will is careful, and the ethics of moral responsibility under determinism is treated rigorously.

title: Mortality
author: Christopher Hitchens
synopsis: A short collection of essays written during the author's final illness, meditating on death, the ethics of dying, and the philosophical consolations available to the non-religious.
review: A philosophical memoir in the Montaigne tradition. The reflections on the ethics of gratitude and the metaphysics of selfhood under illness are genuinely moving.

title: The Moral Animal
author: Robert Wright
synopsis: An evolutionary psychology book arguing for a Darwinian foundation to moral philosophy, drawing on the ethics of reciprocity, the philosophy of the self, and the phenomenology of social life.
review: Popular philosophy of mind with a strong evolutionary argument. The chapters on the ethics of honesty and the metaphysics of the evolved self are the philosophical core of the book.

title: Think
author: Simon Blackburn
synopsis: An introductory textbook covering the core problems of philosophy: knowledge, mind, free will, ethics, god, and the metaphysics of the self, in short thematic chapters.
review: A clear introduction to analytic philosophy. Blackburn's chapters on epistemology, the mind-body problem, and the ethics of consequentialism are model popular philosophy.

title: Philosophy: The Basics
author: Nigel Warburton
synopsis: An introductory textbook covering the core problems of philosophy: the existence of god, ethics, political philosophy, the external world, science, mind, and art.
review: A clean introductory philosophy text. Each chapter sets up the problem, surveys the classical arguments, and leaves the reader with a map of the field rather than a conclusion.

title: The Discourses
author: Niccolò Machiavelli
synopsis: A longer work of political philosophy drawing lessons from the Roman historian Livy, covering republican government, the ethics of political action, and the virtues of citizens.
review: A philosophical companion to The Prince that is substantially more republican in spirit. The ethics of political violence and the virtues of mixed government are the main themes.

title: The Dalai Lama's Little Book of Inner Peace
author: Dalai Lama
synopsis: A short compendium of Buddhist teachings on ethics, compassion, and the philosophy of everyday mental life, aimed at a general Western audience.
review: Popular Buddhist philosophy. Short chapters on the ethics of anger and the phenomenology of compassion that will be familiar to anyone who has read the author before.

title: On Grand Strategy
author: John Lewis Gaddis
synopsis: A historian's survey of strategic thinking across two and a half millennia, from Herodotus and Thucydides through Augustine, Machiavelli, Lincoln, and the twentieth-century cold war.
review: Military and diplomatic history in the Liddell Hart tradition. The chapters on the Peloponnesian war and the Spanish Armada are the strongest historical set pieces.

title: The Great Transformation
author: Karl Polanyi
synopsis: A classic economic history of the nineteenth century arguing that the self-regulating market was a political creation, not a natural order, and tracing its collapse into the wars of the twentieth.
review: Economic history as political argument. Polanyi's archival work on the Speenhamland system and the gold standard remains essential to economic historians.

title: Stumbling on Happiness
author: Daniel Gilbert
synopsis: A popular philosophy and psychology book on why humans are systematically wrong about what will make them happy, drawing on cognitive science and moral philosophy.
review: Popular philosophy of mind with a strong experimental backbone. The chapters on affective forecasting and the ethics of imagined futures are philosophically interesting beyond the psychology.

title: Scars
author: Chris Wraight
synopsis: A Horus Heresy novel following the White Scars legion and their primarch Jaghatai Khan as they agonise over whether to side with the loyalist Emperor or the traitor Warmaster.
review: Wraight writes the White Scars with nomadic grace. The primarch's long decision, the legion's internal debate, and the warp-tinged attacks on their homeworld make this a philosophically serious Heresy novel.

title: Vulkan Lives
author: Nick Kyme
synopsis: A Horus Heresy novel about the tortured primarch Vulkan of the Salamanders legion, held captive by a traitor primarch and repeatedly killed by daemonic chaos rituals.
review: Kyme's Vulkan is the emotional heart of this Heresy entry. The daemonic torture, the loyalist space marine rescue operation, and the chaos warp weapons all come together in a grim piece of tie-in fiction.

title: Praetorian of Dorn
author: John French
synopsis: A Horus Heresy novel about the Imperial Fists primarch Rogal Dorn defending the Sol system against an Alpha Legion traitor infiltration deep inside the loyalist heartland.
review: French writes political thriller inside the 40k universe. The Alpha Legion infiltrators, the loyalist primarch's paranoia, and the legion-on-legion warp combat in the Sol system give the novel real tension.

title: For the Emperor
author: Sandy Mitchell
synopsis: A Warhammer 40k novel following a cowardly commissar through an imperial guard campaign against traitor forces and tau incursions on a backwater planet.
review: Mitchell writes 40k as comedy without losing the chaos-haunted grimdark backdrop. The commissar's unwanted heroism, the imperial guard regiment, and the warp-touched enemies give the novel a lighter but still unmistakably 40k tone.

title: Eisenhorn
author: Dan Abnett
synopsis: An omnibus of Warhammer 40k novels following an Imperial inquisitor across decades of investigations into chaos cults, xenos artefacts, and the corruption of the Imperium's own nobility.
review: Abnett's best 40k work. The inquisitor's slide toward radical heresy over three novels is one of the most satisfying character arcs in tie-in fiction. Chaos daemons, warp cultists, and rogue space marines all pass through the story.

title: Gaunt's Ghosts: First and Only
author: Dan Abnett
synopsis: A Warhammer 40k military novel following an Imperial Guard regiment of stealth troopers through a bitter campaign of attrition on a chaos-held crusade world.
review: Sharpe in space. Abnett's Imperial Guard novel trades space marine spectacle for trench warfare, mud, traitor guardsmen, and warp-tainted enemies. The best entry point to 40k for readers who dislike power armour.

title: The Forever War
author: Joe Haldeman
synopsis: A military science fiction novel following a conscript soldier across centuries of relativistic interstellar warfare against an alien species, in which each deployment lands him further out of step with the society he fights for.
review: A classic of military science fiction. The campaign-level combat is handled well but the novel's real subject is the alienation of a soldier across centuries. Fiction that owes as much to Vietnam as to Heinlein.

title: Old Man's War
author: John Scalzi
synopsis: A science fiction novel in which the elderly of Earth are enlisted into an interstellar military and given new bodies to fight an endless war of colonial expansion against hostile alien species.
review: Scalzi's debut is a warm and readable updating of Starship Troopers. The campaign structure, the basic training chapters, and the alien enemies all sit squarely in the military SF tradition.

title: Nudge
author: Richard H. Thaler and Cass R. Sunstein
synopsis: A behavioural science book on choice architecture, drawing on experimental research into how default settings and framing effects shape decisions about savings, health, and public policy.
review: A landmark in behavioural economics. Thaler and Sunstein summarise decades of experimental research into cognitive biases and present the evidence for nudge-based interventions. The studies are well chosen and the data is accessibly presented.

title: Predictably Irrational
author: Dan Ariely
synopsis: A behavioural science book reporting the author's experimental studies on cognitive biases, anchoring, and the systematic ways humans deviate from rational choice theory.
review: Experimental psychology at its most readable. Ariely reports his own research designs and the data from each study, and the cumulative evidence for predictable irrationality is hard to ignore.

title: The Undoing Project
author: Michael Lewis
synopsis: A scientific biography of the psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, covering the experiments, data, and theoretical papers that founded behavioural economics and prospect theory.
review: A history of the research programme that produced prospect theory. Lewis reconstructs the experiments and the statistical reasoning that led to the Nobel, and the scientific collaboration is the real subject.

title: Life 3.0
author: Max Tegmark
synopsis: A physicist's survey of artificial intelligence research, covering the science of machine learning, the theoretical prospect of general intelligence, and the empirical evidence for current capability trends.
review: A serious scientific treatment of AI. Tegmark marshals the research literature on neural networks, the evidence from benchmark studies, and the theoretical physics of computation into a coherent popular science argument.

title: Human Compatible
author: Stuart Russell
synopsis: An AI researcher's scientific argument about the alignment problem, drawing on decades of research in reinforcement learning, decision theory, and the formal study of rational agents.
review: Russell writes as a working scientist. The book is dense with research citations, formal decision-theoretic arguments, and empirical evidence from reinforcement learning experiments. A serious work of AI science.

title: The Knowledge: How to Rebuild Civilisation in the Aftermath of a Cataclysm
author: Lewis Dartnell
synopsis: A practical popular science book on the scientific knowledge a civilisation would need to rebuild itself, covering agriculture, chemistry, metallurgy, medicine, and experimental physics.
review: A tour of applied science. Dartnell explains the experimental evidence behind each technology, from the chemistry of soap to the physics of the internal combustion engine. Rigorous popular science with clear research sourcing.

title: The Drunkard's Walk
author: Leonard Mlodinow
synopsis: A popular statistics book on how randomness shapes everyday life, drawing on the history of probability theory, empirical research in psychology, and data from sports, finance, and medicine.
review: Popular statistics done well. Mlodinow walks through the historical development of probability theory and the experimental evidence that humans systematically misread random data. A good companion to Kahneman.

title: Lifespan: The Science of Aging
author: Nessa Carey
synopsis: A molecular biologist's overview of the scientific research into aging, covering cellular senescence, epigenetic clocks, and the experimental evidence for interventions that extend healthspan.
review: A rigorous scientific survey of aging research. Carey summarises the experimental data from model organisms, the molecular biology of senescence, and the clinical trial evidence for candidate therapeutics.

title: Storm in a Teacup
author: Helen Czerski
synopsis: A physicist's collection of short essays explaining the physics of everyday phenomena, from surface tension in coffee to the fluid dynamics of raindrops, grounded in experimental research.
review: Popular physics in the Feynman tradition. Czerski anchors each essay in a concrete experiment and the underlying research in fluid dynamics, thermodynamics, and optics. Elegant popular science essays.

title: The Man Who Knew Infinity
author: Robert Kanigel
synopsis: A scientific biography of the Indian mathematician Srinivasa Ramanujan and his collaboration with G. H. Hardy, covering the research, the number theory, and the mathematical evidence for his remarkable conjectures.
review: A history of mathematical research at Cambridge. Kanigel takes the reader through the number theory research and the experimental mathematics that Ramanujan practised, with serious attention to the scientific content.

title: Godel's Proof
author: Ernest Nagel and James R. Newman
synopsis: A short popular mathematics book explaining Godel's incompleteness theorems, covering the formal research into mathematical logic, recursive systems, and the consistency of axiomatic theories.
review: A model of mathematical popularisation. The book lays out the research context of Hilbert's programme, the formal statement of the theorems, and the proof sketch with rigour appropriate to the science.

title: Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise
author: Anders Ericsson and Robert Pool
synopsis: A research psychologist's account of the scientific study of expertise, covering decades of experimental evidence on deliberate practice, working memory, and the acquisition of skill.
review: The empirical foundation behind the ten-thousand-hours idea. Ericsson summarises his own research programme on deliberate practice and the studies on chess masters, musicians, and athletes. A serious popular science synthesis.

title: An Immense World
author: Ed Yong
synopsis: A popular biology book on the sensory worlds of animals, drawing on decades of experimental research into echolocation, electroreception, magnetic navigation, and the neuroscience of perception across species.
review: Popular science writing at its most rigorous. Yong synthesises hundreds of research papers and his reporting on active experimental studies in bat echolocation and octopus photoreception is outstanding.
